exercises.name: Barbell Back Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with the bar resting across your upper back, feet about shoulder-width apart and toes turned out slightly. Brace your core, push your knees out in line with your toes, and lower yourself until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor. Drive through your whole foot to stand back up. Don't let your knees cave inward on the way up — that's the most common mistake and it puts a lot of stress on your joints.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-12 reps hypertrophy. Noa: L4-L5 risk if torso angle >35°. Boris: Top set + 2 back-off sets.

exercises.name: Front Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Hold the bar across the front of your shoulders with your elbows up high, feet shoulder-width apart and toes out slightly. Keep your torso as upright as possible as you squat straight down, knees tracking over your toes. Stand back up by driving through your heels. Don't let your elbows drop — once they fall, your chest follows and the bar rolls forward off your shoulders.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, quad-dominant. Noa: Safer for lower back vs back squat. Boris: Moderate weight, focus form.

exercises.name: Goblet Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell vertically at chest height with both hands cupped around the top end. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes out slightly, then squat down keeping your chest tall and elbows inside your knees. Drive back up through your heels. This is a great move for learning squat mechanics — don't rush it, and don't let your chest cave forward.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, great warm-up. Noa: Very safe for L4-L5. Boris: Pump work, slow tempo.

exercises.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Stand a couple of feet in front of a bench and place the top of one foot on it behind you. Lower your back knee straight down toward the floor, keeping your front shin as vertical as possible and your torso upright. Push through your front heel to stand back up. Don't let your front knee drift way past your toes or your torso fold forward — both are signs your stance needs adjusting.
exercises.committeeNotes: Quad and glute focus.

exercises.name: Leg Press
exercises.setupNotes: Sit in the machine with your back flat against the pad and place your feet shoulder-width apart on the platform. Lower the weight by bending your knees until they're at about a 90-degree angle, then press back up without locking your knees out at the top. Keep your lower back pressed into the seat throughout. Never let your lower back peel away from the pad at the bottom — that's a fast track to a back injury.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 reps, high volume. Noa: Safe quad/glute builder if ROM controlled. Boris: Pyramids, partial reps at top.

exercises.name: Hack Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Step into the machine with your back flat against the pad, feet shoulder-width apart on the platform. Lower yourself by bending your knees until your thighs are at or below parallel, then drive back up without locking out at the top. Keep your heels flat on the platform the whole time. Don't let your lower back round away from the pad as you go deeper — shorten your range of motion before you let that happen.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, quad emphasis. Noa: Lower back supported. Boris: Great for intensity techniques.

exercises.name: Safety Bar Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Set the safety bar on your upper back so the camber bar wraps over your shoulders and the handles hang in front of you. Grip the handles lightly for stability, brace your core, and squat down like a normal back squat. The bar's forward weight means you'll naturally stay more upright, so use that to your advantage. Don't death-grip the handles and fight the bar — let your upper body find a comfortable position and focus on leg drive.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-10 reps. Noa: Best squat variant for elbow/shoulder injuries. Boris: Build to heavy singles.

exercises.name: Box Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Set up a box or bench behind you at about parallel height and squat with the bar across your upper back just like a regular back squat. Sit back and down onto the box with control, pause briefly, then drive hard through your heels to stand back up. Keep your chest up and don't relax completely when you touch the box — stay tight. Don't slam down onto the box or use it as a resting point; the pause should be controlled, not a flop.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 3-6 reps, power focus. Noa: Controlled depth protects L4-L5. Boris: Speed work, 50-70% 1RM.

exercises.name: Pause Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Perform a standard back squat but hold the bottom position for 2–3 seconds before standing back up. Keep every muscle braced during the pause — chest up, knees out, core tight. This kills the stretch reflex, so you'll need less weight than usual, and that's totally fine. Don't relax or shift your weight during the pause — think of it as an isometric hold, not a rest.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 4-8 reps, strength phase. Noa: Great for teaching depth control. Boris: 70-85% 1RM pauses.

exercises.name: Sumo Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Take a wide stance with your toes pointed out significantly — wider than your hips — and hold the bar across your upper back. Squat straight down, driving your knees out hard in line with your toes, until your thighs reach parallel or below. Stand back up by squeezing your glutes and pushing the floor away. Don't let your knees collapse inward — with a wide stance, knee cave is even more of a risk and requires conscious effort to prevent.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, glute/adductor emphasis. Noa: Alternative if conventional hurts. Boris: Moderate weight, volume.

exercises.name: Conventional Deadlift
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with the bar over the middle of your feet, grip just outside your legs at shoulder width. Take a big breath, brace your core, and pull the bar by driving your feet through the floor while keeping your chest up and back straight. Lock out at the top by squeezing your glutes, then lower the bar with control. Don't let your lower back round as the bar leaves the floor — if your back rounds before the bar breaks the ground, the weight is too heavy.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 3-8 reps. Noa: HIGH L4-L5 RISK - monitor closely. Boris: Build singles, limit volume.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift (RDL) - BB
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a barbell in front of your thighs with a shoulder-width grip, standing tall with a slight bend in your knees. Hinge at your hips and push them back as you lower the bar down your legs, keeping your back straight and the bar close to your body. Stop when you feel a strong stretch in the back of your thighs, then drive your hips forward to stand back up. Don't bend your knees more as the weight gets heavy — the movement is a hip hinge, not a squat.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, hamstring hypertrophy. Noa: Moderate L4-L5 risk, watch form. Boris: Controlled tempo.

exercises.name: Trap Bar Deadlift
exercises.setupNotes: Stand inside the trap bar with feet hip-width apart, hinge down and grab both handles. Keep your back straight, push your chest out, and drive through your whole foot to stand up tall. Lower the bar back down with control by hinging at the hips. Don't let your lower back round at the start of the pull — if the weight is too heavy to lift with a straight back, go lighter.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 5-10 reps, safer alternative. Noa: MUCH safer for L4-L5 and elbows. Boris: Great for high-rep work.

exercises.name: Sumo Deadlift
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with a very wide stance, toes pointed out, and grip the bar with both hands inside your legs about shoulder-width apart. Sit your hips down, keep your chest up and back straight, then drive your feet through the floor and push your hips forward to stand up. Keep the bar dragging up your inner legs the whole way. Don't let your hips shoot up before your chest — that turns it into a stiff-leg deadlift and loses all the leg drive.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 3-8 reps. Noa: Better L4-L5 loading vs conventional. Boris: Test max variation.

exercises.name: Single-Leg RDL
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in one or both hands and stand on one leg with a slight bend in the knee. Hinge forward at the hip, letting the free leg float back behind you as a counterweight, and lower the dumbbell toward the floor. Keep your back flat and hips square, then squeeze your glute to drive back up to standing. Don't let your hips rotate open to the side — keep them level the whole way down and up.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps each leg. Noa: Minimal spinal load, safe unilateral. Boris: Stability work.

exercises.name: Deficit Deadlift
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on a small plate or platform (usually 1–2 inches) so the bar starts lower relative to your body than a standard deadlift. Set up and pull exactly like a conventional deadlift, but expect more range of motion through your legs and hips. Keep your back straight and drive through the floor — the extra depth makes the start harder. Don't let the added range trick you into rounding your back — if your back rounds at the start, step off the deficit and build up to it.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 3-6 reps, strength work. Noa: HIGH RISK - only with perfect form. Boris: Overload stretch, careful.

exercises.name: Rack Pull
exercises.setupNotes: Set the bar on pins or safety bars in a rack at around knee height, grip it at shoulder width and stand tall. Brace your core and pull the bar by driving your hips through, locking out at the top. This is a partial deadlift that lets you overload the top half of the pull. Don't use the shortened range as an excuse to go too heavy too soon — your lower back is still under a lot of load.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 3-8 reps, overload. Noa: Safer partial ROM for back. Boris: Supramax loading.

exercises.name: Stiff-Leg Deadlift
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a barbell at hip height with legs nearly straight — just a very small bend in the knees — and hinge from the hips to lower the bar toward the floor. Keep your back flat and the bar close to your legs throughout the movement. Stand back up by squeezing your glutes and driving your hips forward. Don't round your lower back to reach the floor — lower only as far as your flexibility honestly allows.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, hamstring focus. Noa: Moderate L4-L5 risk. Boris: Light-moderate weight.

exercises.name: Barbell Flat Bench Press
exercises.setupNotes: Lie flat on the bench with your eyes under the bar. Unrack the bar and lower it to your mid-chest, keeping your elbows at roughly a 45–75° angle from your body. Press straight up until your arms are locked out, then repeat. Don't let your lower back arch so much that your hips lift off the bench — keep your feet flat and your butt down.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-12 reps. Noa: 45° elbow angle protects medial epicondyle. Boris: Comp lift, build technique.

exercises.name: Incline Barbell Bench Press
exercises.setupNotes: Set the bench to a 30–45° incline and lie back so the bar starts above your upper chest. Lower the bar to just below your collar bones, pause briefly, then press back up. Keep your shoulder blades pulled together and your wrists stacked over your elbows throughout. Don't set the incline too steep — beyond 45° you're mostly doing shoulders, not chest.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, clavicular pec. Noa: Elbow-safe pressing angle. Boris: Moderate weight, volume.

exercises.name: Decline Barbell Bench Press
exercises.setupNotes: Hook your feet under the pads and lie on the decline bench so your head is lower than your hips. Lower the bar to your lower chest, pause, then press up. Your grip can be slightly closer than flat bench. Don't rush the descent — control the bar on the way down to avoid shoulder strain.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-10 reps, lower pec. Noa: Reduced shoulder stress. Boris: Heavy pressing variation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Flat Bench Press
exercises.setupNotes: Lie flat on the bench, holding a dumbbell in each hand at chest level with palms facing your feet. Press both dumbbells up until your arms are straight, then lower them under control until your elbows drop just below bench height. Let the dumbbells travel slightly inward at the top so they're almost touching. Don't let your elbows flare out to 90° — keep a slight tuck to protect your shoulders.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, hypertrophy. Noa: Safer for elbows than barbell. Boris: Drop sets, volume work.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.setupNotes: Set the bench to about 30–45° and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs. Kick them up to your shoulders and press them up above your upper chest. Lower them with control until you feel a good stretch in your chest, then drive back up. Don't bounce the dumbbells off your chest or lose tension at the bottom.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, upper chest. Noa: Excellent for elbow health. Boris: Pyramids, slow eccentric.

exercises.name: Cable Chest Fly
exercises.setupNotes: Set both pulleys to chest height and stand in the center of the cable station, holding one handle in each hand. Step forward slightly, hinge at the hips a little, and bring both hands forward and together in a wide arc in front of your chest — like you're hugging a tree. Slowly return them back to the start with control. Don't let your arms straighten completely at the end; keep a soft bend in the elbow to protect the joint.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, isolation. Noa: Very elbow-safe, constant tension. Boris: Pump work, drop sets.

exercises.name: Machine Chest Press
exercises.setupNotes: Sit in the machine and adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest level. Grip the handles and press forward until your arms are almost straight, then return slowly to the start. Keep your back flat against the pad and your feet planted on the floor. Don't let the weight stack touch down between reps — keep tension on your chest throughout the set.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 reps. Noa: Very safe, supported. Boris: Finisher, pump sets.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Bench Press
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on a flat bench and grip the bar with your hands about shoulder-width apart (or slightly narrower). Lower the bar to your lower chest, keeping your elbows tucked close to your sides, then press back up. The close grip shifts work to the triceps compared to a normal bench press. Don't go too narrow — hands inside shoulder-width is enough; going very narrow strains the wrists.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, triceps. Noa: Monitor elbow - may aggravate medial epicondylitis. Boris: Accessory for lockout.

exercises.name: Push-Ups
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a high plank position with hands just wider than shoulder-width and your body forming a straight line from head to heels. Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows at roughly 45° from your sides, then push back up. Keep your core tight so your hips don't sag or pike. Don't let your neck jut forward — your chin should be a couple of inches from the floor at the bottom, not your face.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-30 reps, endurance. Noa: Safe pressing, scalable. Boris: Warm-up, finisher.

exercises.name: Barbell Row
exercises.setupNotes: Hinge forward until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor, knees slightly bent, holding the bar with an overhand grip at shoulder width. Pull the bar into your lower chest or upper stomach, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top. Lower the bar back down with control and repeat. Don't use your lower back to heave the weight — if you're jerking upright to get the bar moving, go lighter.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-12 reps, lat/rhomboid. Noa: L4-L5 risk if form breaks, elbow stress with supination. Boris: Build strength, moderate volume.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Row
exercises.setupNotes: Place one hand and knee on a bench for support, hold a dumbbell in the other hand and let it hang straight down. Pull the dumbbell up toward your hip, driving your elbow back and squeezing your shoulder blade at the top. Lower it all the way back to a full stretch and repeat. Don't rotate your whole torso to get the dumbbell up — the movement should come from your arm and back, not a body twist.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps each side. Noa: Excellent for elbow safety, unilateral. Boris: Moderate-heavy, strict form.

exercises.name: Chest-Supported Row
exercises.setupNotes: Set the chest pad to a height that lets you lie comfortably with arms hanging down to the handles. Pull the handles toward you by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades together. Lower with control to get a full stretch at the bottom before the next rep. Because your chest is supported, you can't cheat with your lower back — use this to really focus on pure back contraction.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps. Noa: SAFEST rowing - no L4-L5 stress. Boris: Volume work, pump.

exercises.name: Cable Row
exercises.setupNotes: Sit at the cable row station with feet on the platform, knees slightly bent, and grab the handle. Keep your torso upright and pull the handle into your stomach, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the end. Let the weight pull your arms forward for a full stretch before the next rep. Don't rock your torso back and forth — the movement should come from your arms and back, not your lower back swinging.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, mid-back. Noa: L4-L5 safe if upright. Boris: Drop sets, time under tension.

exercises.name: T-Bar Row
exercises.setupNotes: Straddle the bar with feet shoulder-width apart, hinge forward to about a 45-degree angle, and grip the handles or a V-bar attachment under the bar. Pull the bar toward your chest by driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades. Lower with control and repeat. Don't stand more upright as the weight gets heavier — that shifts the load to your lower back and away from your lats.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps. Noa: Moderate L4-L5 risk, better elbow position. Boris: Heavy rowing variation.

exercises.name: Seal Row
exercises.setupNotes: Lie face-down on a high bench with the bar hanging below you, grip it at shoulder width. Pull the bar straight up toward the bench, squeezing your shoulder blades hard at the top. Lower it all the way back to a dead-hang stretch before the next rep. Because your body is supported, there is zero room to cheat — enjoy the strict range and really milk that stretch at the bottom.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps. Noa: SAFEST heavy rowing - zero spinal load. Boris: Strength work without fatigue.

exercises.name: Pendlay Row
exercises.setupNotes: Set up like a barbell row with your torso parallel to the floor, but start every rep with the bar resting on the floor. Explosively pull the bar to your lower chest, let it return all the way back to the floor, and reset before each rep. This eliminates momentum and makes each rep a dead-stop pull. Don't bounce the bar off the floor — it should come to a complete stop and then you pull again from scratch.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 5-8 reps, power. Noa: HIGH L4-L5 RISK - only if healthy. Boris: Strength/power phase.

exercises.name: Inverted Row
exercises.setupNotes: Set a bar in a rack at about waist height, grip it overhand at shoulder width and hang underneath it with arms straight and body in a straight line. Pull your chest up to the bar by driving your elbows back, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top. Lower back down slowly. Don't let your hips sag — keep your core tight and your body in a straight plank from heels to head.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 reps. Noa: Very safe, no equipment. Boris: Warm-up, endurance.

exercises.name: Standing Barbell Overhead Press
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with the bar at collarbone height, gripping it just outside shoulder-width. Brace your core, squeeze your glutes, and press the bar straight up overhead, finishing with the bar over the back of your skull. Lower it back to your collarbone in a controlled path. Don't lean back excessively to get the bar up — if you need to, the weight is too heavy.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 5-10 reps, strength. Noa: Shoulder/elbow stress - monitor. Boris: Top set + back-offs.

exercises.name: Seated Dumbbell Overhead Press
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on an upright bench and hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder level, palms facing forward. Press both dumbbells up and slightly together until your arms are straight overhead, then lower them back down. Keep your lower back gently against the pad throughout. Don't let your elbows drift behind your body at the bottom — they should stay directly under your wrists.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps. Noa: Safer than standing, supported spine. Boris: Hypertrophy work.

exercises.name: Arnold Press
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on a bench holding dumbbells in front of your shoulders with palms facing you, like the top of a curl. As you press up, rotate your palms outward so they face forward at the top. Reverse the rotation on the way back down. Don't rush the rotation — the palm turn is what makes the Arnold different and more complete for shoulder development.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, complete delt. Noa: Monitor elbow during rotation. Boris: Moderate weight, pump.

exercises.name: Machine Shoulder Press
exercises.setupNotes: Adjust the seat so the handles are at shoulder height and sit back against the pad. Grip the handles and press up until your arms are extended, then return slowly. Keep your core tight and avoid letting your lower back arch off the pad. Don't shrug your shoulders up as you press — keep them pulled down away from your ears.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 reps. Noa: Very safe, supported. Boris: Finisher, metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Landmine Press
exercises.setupNotes: Stand facing the landmine with your feet staggered or shoulder-width apart. Hold the end of the bar at shoulder height with one or both hands. Press the bar up and forward in an arc until your arm(s) are fully extended, then lower it back. The arc means this isn't a straight vertical press — follow the natural path of the bar. Don't lean sideways when pressing one arm — keep your torso upright and core tight.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps. Noa: Shoulder-friendly angle. Boris: Great accessory.

exercises.name: Pike Push-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Get into a downward dog position — hips high, body forming an inverted V. Bend your elbows and lower the top of your head toward the floor, then push back up. The closer your feet are to your hands, the more vertical the press and the harder it is. Don't let your hips drop during the movement — keep them high throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 reps. Noa: Safe pressing pattern. Boris: Warm-up, finisher.

exercises.name: Pull-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a bar with an overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width, arms fully extended. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar by driving your elbows down toward your hips. Lower back down to a full hang before the next rep. Don't kip or swing to get yourself up — controlled, dead-hang pull-ups build far more strength than sloppy momentum reps.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 5-15 reps, lat hypertrophy. Noa: Elbow-safe with pronated grip. Boris: Weight added if >12 reps.

exercises.name: Chin-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a bar with an underhand grip at about shoulder width, arms fully extended. Pull yourself up until your chin is over the bar, then lower back down to a full hang. The underhand grip puts your biceps in a stronger position, making this a great choice if pull-ups feel too hard. Don't cross your ankles and swing — keep your body as still as possible and let your back and arms do the work.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-12 reps, bicep/lat. Noa: May aggravate elbow injury. Boris: Build to weighted.

exercises.name: Neutral Grip Pull-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from parallel handles with palms facing each other and arms fully extended. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the handles or your chest approaches them, then lower with control. The neutral grip is the most joint-friendly variation and often feels the strongest. Don't shrug your shoulders up toward your ears — depress them slightly before you pull to keep the right muscles in charge.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-15 reps. Noa: BEST grip for elbow safety. Boris: Primary variation for high volume.

exercises.name: Lat Pulldown
exercises.setupNotes: Sit with your thighs under the pads, grip the bar wider than shoulder width with an overhand grip. Pull the bar down to your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back, squeezing your lats at the bottom. Let the bar rise back up to a full arm extension before the next rep. Don't pull the bar behind your neck — that puts unnecessary stress on your cervical spine; always pull to the front.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, lat width. Noa: Safe alternative to pull-ups. Boris: Drop sets, volume.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.setupNotes: Sit at the pulldown station and use a close grip attachment with palms facing each other. Pull the handle down toward your upper chest, elbows driving back and in, squeezing your lats hard at the bottom. Let your arms fully extend at the top before each rep. Don't lean way back to heave the weight down — a slight lean is fine but keep it controlled and intentional.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, mid-back. Noa: Very safe grip. Boris: Pump work.

exercises.name: Straight-Arm Pulldown
exercises.setupNotes: Stand facing a high cable with arms extended and grip a straight bar or rope at roughly eye height. Keep your arms nearly straight and pull the bar down in an arc to your thighs, squeezing your lats hard at the bottom. Slowly let the bar rise back up with control. Don't bend your elbows to help pull — straight arms is the whole point; the moment you bend your elbows, your biceps take over.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, isolation. Noa: Safe lat work. Boris: Pre-exhaust or finisher.

exercises.name: Assisted Pull-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Kneel or stand on the platform and grip the bar at shoulder width or slightly wider. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar, lower back down with control. The platform counterbalances some of your body weight — reduce the assist over time as you get stronger. Don't use so much assistance that it feels effortless — you should still have to work hard; use the minimum assist needed.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps. Noa: Good for building strength safely. Boris: Progress to bodyweight.

exercises.name: Lying Leg Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Lie face-down on the machine with the pad resting just above your heels, gripping the handles lightly. Curl your heels toward your glutes as far as they'll go, squeeze at the top for a beat, then lower slowly. Keep your hips pressed down into the pad throughout. Don't let your hips lift off the pad to get more range — that shifts the work away from your hamstrings.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 reps, hamstring isolation. Noa: Safe, no spinal load. Boris: Drop sets, pump.

exercises.name: Seated Leg Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Sit upright with the pad resting on your lower legs just above the ankles, and the top pad securing your thighs. Curl your legs down as far as the machine allows, squeeze your hamstrings hard at the bottom, then return slowly. Keep your back flat against the pad and don't use momentum. Don't let the weight stack slam down on each rep — control the return and keep tension on your hamstrings the whole way up.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps. Noa: Very safe, supported. Boris: Pump work.

exercises.name: Nordic Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Kneel on a padded surface and have a partner hold your ankles down firmly, or anchor them under something heavy. Keeping your body in a straight line from knees to head, lower yourself toward the floor as slowly as possible by letting your knees straighten. Catch yourself at the bottom and use a small push to get back up, then pull yourself back to the start with your hamstrings. Don't let your hips break — your body should move as one rigid plank; hip bending is just cheating yourself.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 3-8 reps, strength. Noa: Injury prevention gold standard. Boris: Negatives if can't do full.

exercises.name: Leg Extension
exercises.setupNotes: Sit upright in the machine with the pad resting on your lower legs just above the ankles and your knees at the edge of the seat. Extend your legs until they're straight, hold for a beat at the top, then lower back down with control. Keep your back against the seat and don't use a swinging motion to lift the weight. Don't lock your knees out aggressively at the top — extend fully but smoothly, especially if you have any knee issues.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, quad isolation. Noa: Can stress patella - monitor knees. Boris: Pre-exhaust or finisher.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Step onto the platform with the balls of your feet on the edge and the shoulder pads resting on your traps. Lower your heels as far down as comfortable to get a good stretch, then rise up onto the balls of your feet as high as possible. Pause at the top and squeeze before lowering. Don't bounce out of the bottom — that skips the stretch reflex and removes the most effective part of the rep.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-30 reps, high volume. Noa: Safe isolation. Boris: Straight sets, pump.

exercises.name: Seated Calf Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Sit with the pads resting just above your knees and the balls of your feet on the platform edge. Lower your heels for a full stretch at the bottom, then push up onto the balls of your feet as high as you can. Pause and squeeze at the top before each rep. Calves are stubborn — go through the full range every rep and don't rush; half-reps won't build them.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-30 reps. Noa: Safe, seated. Boris: Superset with standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Stand holding a barbell with an underhand grip just outside your hips. Pin your elbows to your sides and curl the bar up to shoulder height by bending your elbows, then lower slowly. Your upper arms should stay still — only your forearms move. Don't swing your torso back to help lift the bar; if you're swinging, the weight is too heavy.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps. Noa: May aggravate elbow injury. Boris: Moderate weight, strict form.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand with your palms facing forward. Keeping your elbows pinned to your sides, curl both dumbbells up toward your shoulders, then lower them slowly. You can do both arms at once or alternate. Don't let your elbows drift forward as you approach the top — keep them directly under your shoulders.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps. Noa: Safer than barbell for elbows. Boris: Volume work.

exercises.name: Hammer Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand with your palms facing each other (neutral grip, like holding hammers). Keeping your elbows at your sides, curl both dumbbells up toward your shoulders, then lower slowly. This grip hits the brachialis and brachioradialis more than a standard curl. Don't rotate your wrists during the movement — keep the neutral grip throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps. Noa: BEST curl for medial epicondylitis. Boris: Primary bicep work.

exercises.name: Cable Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a straight bar or EZ attachment to a low pulley. Stand facing the machine, grip the bar underhand, and curl it up toward your shoulders while keeping your elbows stationary. Lower slowly against the cable's resistance. The cable provides constant tension at the bottom of the movement, unlike free weights. Don't let the cable pull your elbows forward — keep them pinned.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, pump. Noa: Moderate elbow stress. Boris: Finisher, drop sets.

exercises.name: Tricep Pushdown
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a bar or rope to a high pulley. Stand facing the machine and grip the attachment with an overhand grip. Keeping your elbows pinned to your sides, push the bar or rope down until your arms are fully extended, then return slowly. Only your forearms should move. Don't lean heavily over the bar to push it down — that turns it into a lat pulldown.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps. Noa: Monitor elbow - moderate stress. Boris: Drop sets, pump.

exercises.name: Overhead Tricep Extension
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell or EZ bar overhead with both hands. Lower it behind your head by bending your elbows, keeping your upper arms vertical and close to your head, then extend back up. Keep your elbows pointed at the ceiling throughout. Don't let your elbows flare out to the sides — that reduces tricep activation and stresses the shoulders.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, long head. Noa: Elbow/shoulder stress - monitor. Boris: Moderate weight.

exercises.name: Skull Crusher
exercises.setupNotes: Lie flat on a bench holding an EZ bar or barbell with an overhand grip, arms extended above your chest. Lower the bar toward your forehead (or just behind your head) by bending only your elbows, then press back up. Your upper arms should stay vertical and still. Don't lower the bar too close to your face — just behind or to the forehead is safer for the elbows.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps. Noa: HIGH elbow risk - avoid if injured. Boris: EZ-bar reduces strain.

exercises.name: Dips
exercises.setupNotes: Grip the parallel bars and lift yourself up, then lean your torso slightly forward and bend your knees. Lower yourself until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor, feeling a stretch across your chest, then push back up. The more you lean forward, the more chest you hit; staying upright shifts the focus to triceps. Don't dip so deep that your shoulders round forward — stop at parallel.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-15 reps. Noa: High shoulder/elbow stress. Boris: Weight added if >12 reps.

exercises.name: Lateral Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides. Raise both arms out to the sides until they reach shoulder height, keeping a slight bend in your elbows, then lower slowly. Lead with your elbows, not your hands, and think about pouring water out of the dumbbells at the top. Don't use momentum or hike your shoulders — the movement should be slow and controlled.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, side delts. Noa: Safe isolation. Boris: High rep, pump work.

exercises.name: Face Pull
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a rope to a cable at face height. Grip the rope with both hands, step back, and pull the rope toward your face — splitting the rope apart as you pull so your hands end up beside your ears. Squeeze your rear delts at the end of the movement, then return slowly. Don't pull with your biceps — focus on driving your elbows back and out to the sides.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps. Noa: ESSENTIAL for shoulder health. Boris: Every session.

exercises.name: Rear Delt Fly
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a pair of light dumbbells and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor. Let the dumbbells hang in front of you, then raise them out to the sides in a wide arc until they reach shoulder height. Keep a slight bend in your elbows. Don't swing the weight up — if you're using momentum, the dumbbells are too heavy.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps. Noa: Safe, posture-improving. Boris: High rep work.

exercises.name: Shrugs
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, standing tall with arms straight. Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible, pause at the top, then lower slowly. Keep your neck relaxed and don't tuck your chin. Don't roll your shoulders — straight up and straight down is the right path; rolling adds rotational stress without adding any benefit.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, trap mass. Noa: Safe isolation. Boris: Heavy weight, volume.

exercises.name: Plank
exercises.setupNotes: Start face-down with your forearms on the floor, elbows under your shoulders, and toes tucked. Lift your hips so your body forms a straight line from head to heels, and brace your stomach like you're about to take a punch. Hold that position, breathing steadily, without letting your hips sag or pike. Don't let your lower back cave — if your hips are dropping, drop to your knees and rebuild the brace before going full.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 30-90 sec holds. Noa: CRITICAL for L4-L5 stability. Boris: Every session.

exercises.name: Ab Wheel Rollout
exercises.setupNotes: Kneel on the floor holding the ab wheel handles with both hands directly below your shoulders. Brace hard, then slowly roll the wheel forward, letting your hips hinge and your arms extend out in front of you — go only as far as you can control. Pull back by driving your stomach in and dragging the wheel back to the start. Don't let your lower back arch on the way out — if you feel it loading in your spine instead of your stomach, shorten the range until you're stronger.
exercises.committeeNotes: Elite core tension.

exercises.name: Cable Crunch
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a rope to a high cable pulley, kneel facing the stack, and hold the rope behind your head with both hands. Hinge at your hips slightly, then curl your upper body downward by pulling your elbows toward your knees — think about bringing your chest to your thighs, not just bowing your head. Pause at the bottom, then slowly return to the start under control. Don't yank the weight down using your hip flexors — the movement should come entirely from rounding your upper back.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps, ab mass. Noa: Safe weighted ab work. Boris: Progressive overload.

exercises.name: Russian Twist
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the floor with your knees bent and feet either anchored or lifted a few inches off the ground. Lean back slightly until you feel your stomach working, clasp your hands together (or hold a weight), and rotate your torso side to side, tapping the floor beside each hip. Keep the movement controlled — twist from your ribs, not just your arms. Don't just swing your arms across while your torso stays still; the rotation has to come from your midsection.
exercises.committeeNotes: Oblique focus.

exercises.name: Hanging Leg Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a pull-up bar with a firm overhand grip, shoulders packed down — don't just dangle. From a dead hang, raise your legs by curling your pelvis upward, bringing your thighs at least to parallel (or higher if you can). Lower them slowly back to the start without swinging. Don't use momentum to kick your legs up — if you're swinging, pause at the bottom each rep to kill the swing before going again.
exercises.committeeNotes: Lower ab focus.

exercises.name: Farmer's Walk
exercises.setupNotes: Pick up two heavy dumbbells or kettlebells, one in each hand, and stand tall with your shoulders pulled back. Walk forward at a steady, controlled pace — keep your chest up, core tight, and steps even. Don't shrug or let the weights pull your shoulders forward and down; think about keeping them locked back the whole walk.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 30-60 sec walks. Noa: Excellent functional strength. Boris: Heavy, short distance.

exercises.name: Sled Push
exercises.setupNotes: Load the sled and grip the uprights with arms straight, leaning your body forward at roughly a 45-degree angle. Drive hard through the balls of your feet in short, powerful strides, keeping your core braced and back straight. Push continuously for the target distance or time without stopping. Don't stand too upright — losing that forward lean kills your power and makes the sled feel twice as heavy.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 20-60 sec pushes. Noa: Very safe, no eccentric damage. Boris: Finisher or conditioning.

exercises.name: Sled Pull
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a harness or handles to a loaded sled behind you and face away from it. Walk or drive forward with powerful leg strides, keeping tension on the rope or strap throughout. Lean slightly forward and use your whole leg to push the ground away with each step. Don't take tiny shuffle steps — commit to full strides to get the most out of each push.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 20-60 sec pulls. Noa: Safe, no eccentric. Boris: Conditioning between sets.

exercises.name: Battle Ropes
exercises.setupNotes: Stand facing the anchor point with feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent, holding one end of each rope. Drive the ropes up and down in alternating waves, keeping your arms moving fast while your hips stay low and your core stays braced. You can vary between alternating waves, double waves, or slams. Don't stand straight up and use only your arms — the power should come from your hips and legs driving the movement.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 20-40 sec rounds. Noa: Safe conditioning. Boris: Finisher or warm-up.

exercises.name: Box Jump
exercises.setupNotes: Stand about a foot back from a sturdy box, feet shoulder-width apart. Dip quickly into a quarter-squat and swing your arms, then explode upward landing softly on the box with both feet at the same time. Stand up fully on the box, then step back down one foot at a time — don't jump back down. Don't land stiff-legged — absorb the impact by landing softly in a slight squat, not with straight knees and a thud.
exercises.committeeNotes: Plyometric power.

exercises.name: Medicine Ball Slam
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart holding a medicine ball overhead with both hands, arms nearly straight. Rise onto your toes slightly, then slam the ball into the floor directly in front of you as hard as you can, hinging at the hips and bending your knees as you follow through. Pick the ball up and repeat — the whole movement should be explosive and full-body. Don't slouch or bend at the waist without driving your hips back; this is a hip hinge, not a bow.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps. Noa: Safe explosive work. Boris: Conditioning or warm-up.

exercises.name: Broad Jump
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, bend into a quarter-squat, swing your arms back, then explode forward as far as possible. Land softly on both feet at the same time, absorbing the landing by bending your knees. Aim to cover as much horizontal distance as you can with each jump. Don't land with your legs straight — you need that soft, bent-knee landing to protect your joints and stick it cleanly.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 3-6 reps. Noa: Land correctly - knee safety. Boris: Power testing.

exercises.name: Kettlebell Swing
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and hike the kettlebell back between your legs like a hike pass. Explosively drive your hips forward to swing the bell up to about chest height, letting your arms stay loose — the power comes from your hips, not your arms. Let the bell fall back and hinge again immediately for the next rep. Don't squat the swing — it's a hip hinge, not a squat; your knees should barely bend and your hips should shoot back.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps. Noa: Moderate L4-L5 risk. Boris: Power endurance.

exercises.name: Turkish Get-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back with a kettlebell pressed straight up in your right hand, right knee bent, foot flat on the floor. Your eyes should stay on the bell the entire time. Roll up onto your left forearm, then your left hand, sweep your left leg behind you into a lunge position, then stand up — all while keeping the bell locked overhead. Reverse the sequence to get back down. Don't rush any transition; the get-up is a slow, deliberate movement where control at each step matters more than speed.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 3-5 reps each side. Noa: Shoulder/core strength. Boris: Skill work, light weight.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart. Drive through your heels to lift your hips off the ground until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees. Squeeze your glutes hard at the top and hold for a second before lowering. Don't flare your ribs or hyperextend your lower back at the top — the squeeze should come from your glutes, not your spine arching.
exercises.committeeNotes: Glute isolation.

exercises.name: Hip Thrust
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a bench, roll a barbell across your hips, and plant your feet flat about hip-width apart. Drive through your heels to thrust your hips up until your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees, squeezing your glutes hard at the top. Lower back down with control and repeat. Don't hyperextend your lower back at the top — your hips should rise until your torso is parallel to the floor, not beyond.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps. Noa: Safe, glute-dominant. Boris: Build to heavy loads.

exercises.name: Wall Sit
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with your back flat against a wall and slide down until your thighs are parallel to the floor, like you're sitting in an invisible chair. Keep your feet flat and about hip-width apart, arms relaxed at your sides or on your thighs. Hold the position for the target time, breathing steadily. Don't let your knees creep past your toes or push your lower back away from the wall — both reduce effectiveness and put extra strain on your knees.
exercises.committeeNotes: Isometric quad endurance.

exercises.name: Step-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Stand in front of a sturdy bench or box, step one foot up onto it, then drive through that heel to bring your whole body up. Bring the trailing leg up to stand fully on top, then step back down the same way. Alternate legs or do all reps on one side before switching. Don't push off the back foot to help yourself up — make the working leg do all the lifting.
exercises.committeeNotes: Unilateral leg strength.

exercises.name: Reverse Lunge
exercises.setupNotes: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand, then step one foot back and lower your back knee toward the floor. Keep your front shin vertical and your torso upright, then push through your front heel to return to standing. Alternate legs for each rep. Don't let your front knee cave inward as you push back up — drive it outward to stay aligned.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps each leg. Noa: Knee-friendly lunge. Boris: Volume work.

exercises.name: Walking Lunge
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand and step one foot forward into a lunge, lowering your back knee close to the floor. Push off your back foot and bring it forward past your front foot into the next lunge, walking across the floor. Keep your torso upright and your front knee tracking over your toes on each step. Don't lean forward as you fatigue — the moment your chest drops is the moment you lose the benefit and start stressing your lower back.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 steps total. Noa: Safe unilateral. Boris: Conditioning element.

exercises.name: Sissy Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Stand holding something lightly for balance, feet together or hip-width apart. Lean your whole body back as one unit while bending your knees and rising onto the balls of your feet, lowering until your knees nearly touch the floor. Squeeze your quads to pull back up to the start. Don't let your hips break — your body from knees to shoulders should stay in a straight line throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps. Noa: High knee stress - careful. Boris: Quad finisher.

exercises.name: Good Morning
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a barbell across your upper back like a squat, stand with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in your knees. Hinge forward at the hips, pushing them back as your torso lowers toward parallel with the floor, keeping your back straight. Drive your hips through to return to standing. Don't round your lower back — this exercise puts a lot of load on your spine, so keeping a flat back is non-negotiable.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps. Noa: HIGH L4-L5 RISK - only if healthy. Boris: Accessory, light-moderate.

exercises.name: Preacher Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Sit at the preacher bench and place the backs of your upper arms on the angled pad. Hold an EZ bar or dumbbells with an underhand grip and curl the weight up from a nearly straight-arm position to just below full contraction. Lower slowly all the way down to get a full stretch. Don't swing or bounce the weight at the bottom — lower under full control.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps. Noa: Elbow stress - avoid if injured. Boris: Strict form, peak contraction.

exercises.name: Concentration Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on a bench and lean forward, bracing the back of your upper arm against the inside of your thigh. Hold a dumbbell and curl it up toward your shoulder, then lower slowly. Your thigh acts as a brace to eliminate any swinging. Don't rush — slow, controlled reps isolate the bicep effectively.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps. Noa: Safe isolation. Boris: Finisher, pump.

exercises.name: Incline Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Set a bench to about 45–60°. Lie back against it and let your arms hang straight down, dumbbells in hand. Curl both dumbbells up to shoulder height, then lower slowly. The incline stretches your biceps at the start of each rep, increasing the range of motion. Don't let your shoulders round forward — keep them pressed into the bench throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps. Noa: Moderate elbow stress. Boris: Long head emphasis.

exercises.name: Tricep Kickback
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor. Bring your upper arm up so it's parallel to the floor, then extend your forearm back until your arm is fully straight. Lower and repeat. Keep your upper arm still — only the forearm moves. Don't let your elbow drop during the movement; the upper arm stays horizontal.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps. Noa: Safe, low elbow stress. Boris: Finisher, pump.

exercises.name: Diamond Push-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Get into a push-up position and place your hands close together under your chest, forming a diamond shape with your thumbs and index fingers. Lower your chest toward your hands, keeping your elbows tucked, then push back up. This places heavy emphasis on the triceps. Don't let your elbows flare out — keep them pointing back, not sideways.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 reps. Noa: Moderate elbow stress. Boris: Bodyweight tricep work.

exercises.name: Bench Dips
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the edge of a bench and place your hands beside your hips with your fingers pointing forward. Step your feet out in front and lower yourself by bending your elbows until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor, then push back up. Keep your back close to the bench. Don't dip too deep or let your shoulders roll forward — stop when your upper arms are parallel.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 reps. Noa: Monitor shoulder/elbow depth. Boris: High rep work.

exercises.name: Upright Row
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a barbell (or dumbbells) in front of your thighs with an overhand grip slightly narrower than shoulder-width. Pull the weight straight up along your body until it reaches chin height, keeping your elbows higher than your wrists. Lower slowly. Don't go too narrow with your grip — wide grip reduces shoulder impingement risk.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps. Noa: Shoulder impingement risk - careful. Boris: Moderate weight.

exercises.name: Front Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs. Raise both arms straight in front of you to shoulder height, keeping a slight bend in your elbows, then lower slowly. You can alternate arms or do both together. Don't swing your torso — keep your body still and let your shoulders do the work.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps. Noa: Safe isolation. Boris: Pump work.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a single handle to a low pulley. Stand sideways to the machine and hold the handle with the hand furthest from the machine. Raise your arm out to the side to shoulder height, then lower slowly. The cable provides constant tension throughout the movement, unlike dumbbells. Don't lean away from the machine to cheat the weight up.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps. Noa: Safe, shoulder-friendly. Boris: Drop sets.

exercises.name: Reverse Pec Deck
exercises.setupNotes: Sit facing the machine (backward from normal pec deck use) and grip the handles with your arms out in front. Open your arms wide to the sides, squeezing your rear delts at the end of the movement, then return slowly. Adjust the seat so the handles are at shoulder height. Don't let your lower back arch away from the pad — keep your chest against it.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps. Noa: Safe, supported. Boris: High rep pump.

exercises.name: Adductor Machine
exercises.setupNotes: Sit in the machine with the pads on the inside of your thighs and legs spread to the starting position. Squeeze your knees together against the resistance, pause at the fully closed position, then let the pads open back out with control. Keep your back flat against the pad and don't use momentum. Don't slam the pads together — the squeeze should be controlled and deliberate, and the return just as slow.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps. Noa: Safe, no knee stress. Boris: Accessory work.

exercises.name: Abductor Machine
exercises.setupNotes: Sit in the machine with the pads on the outside of your thighs and legs together at the starting position. Push your knees outward against the resistance, pause at the fully open position, then bring them back together slowly. Sit tall and keep your lower back against the seat. Don't use your lower back to push — if your torso is rocking, the weight is too heavy.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps. Noa: Hip health, injury prevention. Boris: Accessory work.

exercises.name: Donkey Calf Raise
exercises.setupNotes: Lean forward and rest your lower back under the machine's pad or have a partner sit on your lower back — the key is the load being across the hips, not the shoulders. Plant the balls of your feet on the platform edge and do full-range calf raises from deep stretch to full peak. This angle puts a unique stretch on the calves that standing versions can't replicate. Don't skimp on the range — the full deep stretch at the bottom is what makes this variation worthwhile.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 20-40 reps. Noa: Safe, no spinal load. Boris: Pump work.

exercises.name: Wrist Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on a bench with your forearm resting on your thigh or on the bench, hand extended past your knee. Hold a light dumbbell or barbell with an underhand grip and curl your wrist upward, then lower it all the way down. Full range of motion is key. Don't use a heavy weight — this is a small joint movement and wrists can be easily strained.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-25 reps. Noa: Forearm health. Boris: Accessory, grip strength.

exercises.name: Dead Hang
exercises.setupNotes: Jump or step up to a pull-up bar and hang with arms fully extended, feet off the floor. Relax your shoulders slightly and let your whole spine decompress, breathing steadily throughout. Hold for as long as you can — this builds grip strength and shoulder health simultaneously. Don't shrug your shoulders up to your ears — let them gently elevate under the load; that decompression is the whole point.
exercises.committeeNotes: Grip strength and spinal decompression.

exercises.name: Decline Dumbbell Press
exercises.setupNotes: Secure your feet under the decline bench pads and lie back, dumbbells at your lower chest. Press them up until your arms are extended, then lower them slowly back to the start. Keep your wrists neutral and directly above your elbows at the bottom. Don't let the dumbbells drift toward your shoulders — keep the path vertical over your lower chest.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, excellent lower chest isolation. Noa: Lower spinal load than decline BB press. Boris: 3x10-12, use as secondary chest movement.

exercises.name: Pec Deck Fly
exercises.setupNotes: Sit in the machine and place your forearms against the pads (or grip the handles). Start with your arms wide and bring them together in front of you by squeezing your chest. Hold the contracted position for a beat, then return slowly. Don't load this machine too heavy — it puts significant stress on the shoulder joint at full stretch.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, best chest isolation at long muscle length. Noa: Avoid over-stretching if shoulder impingement history. Boris: 3x15-20, finisher.

exercises.name: Cable Crossover
exercises.setupNotes: Set the pulleys high and stand in the center holding both handles. With a slight forward lean and soft elbows, pull both handles down and across your body until they meet in front of your hips or cross over each other. Squeeze your chest hard at the bottom, then return with control. Don't swing or use momentum — the movement should be slow and deliberate.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-15 reps, constant tension advantage over DBs. Noa: Low shoulder stress when cued correctly. Boris: 3x12-15, post-press finisher.

exercises.name: Ring Push-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Set the rings low enough that you can do a push-up from the floor. Grip the rings with your palms facing each other and perform a push-up, allowing the rings to rotate naturally as you lower and press. At the top, turn your palms outward and lock your elbows to maximize chest activation. Don't let the rings wobble wildly — control the instability with your chest and shoulders.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, high pec activation + stabiliser demand. Noa: Avoid if wrist pain; add rings slowly. Boris: 3x8-12, excellent bodyweight progression.

exercises.name: Weighted Push-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Get into a standard push-up position and have a partner place a weight plate on your upper back, or use a weighted vest. Perform a push-up with full range of motion — chest close to the floor at the bottom, arms fully extended at the top. Keep your core braced so your hips don't sag under the extra load. Don't start too heavy — even 10–25 lbs makes a significant difference.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, easily progressive via load. Noa: Core must stay rigid to protect lumbar. Boris: Load weekly, treat like bench press progression.

exercises.name: Smith Machine Bench Press
exercises.setupNotes: Set up a flat bench inside the Smith machine and position the bar at chest height. Lie back so the bar sits directly over your mid-chest, unhook it, and lower it in a controlled straight line before pressing it back up. The fixed bar path means you don't need to fight lateral balance, so you can focus purely on pressing. Don't let the machine path lure you into a full vertical press — still aim the bar slightly toward your upper chest.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-12 reps, good for beginners or rehabbing shoulder. Noa: Fixed bar path reduces rotator cuff demand. Boris: Useful for overload phases.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.setupNotes: Lie perpendicular across a flat bench, with only your upper back on the bench and your hips dropped. Hold one dumbbell with both hands above your chest. Lower it back over your head in an arc, feeling a stretch through your chest and lats, then pull it back up. Don't drop your hips — keeping them low increases the range of motion and chest stretch.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-15 reps, hits pec-lat seam. Noa: High shoulder mobility demand, skip if impingement. Boris: 3x12, good chest finisher.

exercises.name: Decline Push-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Place your feet on an elevated surface (a bench or box) and your hands on the floor in a push-up position. Lower your chest toward the floor, keeping your body in a straight line, then push back up. The elevated feet shift the load toward your upper chest and shoulders. Don't let your hips hike up — keep your core tight so your body stays flat.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-20 reps, increases upper pec load. Noa: Very safe; scalable with box height. Boris: Great warm-up or finisher, add vest for progression.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Chest Fly
exercises.setupNotes: Lie flat on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand, arms extended above your chest with a soft bend in the elbows. Open your arms wide in a wide arc, lowering the dumbbells to chest level until you feel a stretch, then bring them back together above your chest. Keep the slight bend in your elbows constant throughout — don't let them straighten or over-bend. Don't lower the dumbbells below chest height; it puts unnecessary stress on the shoulder joint.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-15 reps, excellent pec stretch. Noa: High shoulder stress at bottom; limit ROM if impingement. Boris: 3x12, post-press accessory.

exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back with your arms pointing straight up toward the ceiling and your knees bent at 90 degrees, shins parallel to the floor. Press your lower back firmly into the ground, then slowly lower your right arm overhead and straighten your left leg toward the floor at the same time, stopping just before either touches. Return to the start and repeat on the other side. Don't let your lower back lift off the floor — if it comes up, you've gone too far and need to shorten the range.
exercises.committeeNotes: Spinal stability.

exercises.name: Pallof Press
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a single handle to a cable stack at chest height and stand side-on to the machine with your feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the handle at your chest with both hands, brace your core, then press your arms straight out in front of you — hold for a second — then bring them back in. The goal is to resist the cable pulling you toward the stack. Don't rotate your torso as you press; your whole job is to stay square while the cable tries to twist you.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, top anti-rotation exercise. Noa: Extremely safe, no spinal flexion. Boris: 3x10/side, essential for lifters with rotation deficits.

exercises.name: Dragon Flag
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on a flat bench and grip the bench behind your head with both hands, arms straight. Brace everything and lift your entire body off the bench in one rigid line, supported only on your upper back. Lower your body slowly toward the bench, keeping it perfectly straight, then drive back up. Don't pike at the hips as you lower — if your hips bend, your lower back is taking over and the exercise loses its purpose.
exercises.committeeNotes: Elite anti-extension.

exercises.name: Landmine Rotation
exercises.setupNotes: Load one end of a barbell into a landmine attachment (or a corner). Stand facing the end with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, holding the end of the bar with both hands at chest height, arms extended. Rotate the bar down and across your body to one hip, then swing it back up and across to the other hip in a smooth arc — let your hips rotate with the movement. Don't use only your arms; your hips and thoracic spine need to open up to drive the arc.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15/side, rotational power + oblique strength. Noa: Moderate spinal load, safe when controlled. Boris: 3x10/side, great for athletes.

exercises.name: Hollow Body Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back and press your lower back flat into the floor. Extend your arms overhead and straighten your legs, then lift both your shoulders and legs off the floor until only your lower back is in contact with the ground. Hold this banana-shaped position while breathing steadily. Don't let your lower back lift — if it arches up, bend your knees or raise your legs higher until you can hold a flat back.
exercises.committeeNotes: Foundational gymnastics core strength.

exercises.name: Side Plank
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your side and prop yourself up on one forearm, elbow directly under your shoulder. Stack your feet or stagger them for more stability, then lift your hips so your body forms a straight diagonal line from head to feet. Hold without letting your hip drop toward the floor. Don't let your top hip rotate forward or your bottom hip sag — both are signs your lateral muscles have checked out.
exercises.committeeNotes: Hold for time. Excellent for obliques.

exercises.name: Bicycle Crunch
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back, hands loosely behind your head, knees bent and feet off the floor. Bring one knee toward your chest while rotating your opposite elbow to meet it, then switch sides in a smooth pedaling motion. Keep your lower back pressed into the floor throughout. Don't pull on your neck or crank your head from side to side — the rotation should come from your ribcage, not your cervical spine.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 15-20/side, high oblique EMG. Noa: Controlled speed prevents neck strain. Boris: 3x15/side, excellent oblique accessory.

exercises.name: L-Sit
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the floor (or two parallel bars/handles) with your hands planted beside your hips. Press through your palms to lift your body off the surface, then extend your legs straight out in front of you so they're parallel to the floor. Hold that position without letting your legs drop. Don't bend your knees to make it easier — a tucked L-sit is a valid progression, but the full version requires perfectly straight legs.
exercises.committeeNotes: High core and hip flexor demand.

exercises.name: Landmine Anti-Rotation Press
exercises.setupNotes: Load a barbell into a landmine and stand side-on to it, holding the end of the bar at chest height with both hands. Press the bar forward and slightly away from you while resisting the rotational pull — hold for a second — then bring it back. Your torso should stay completely square throughout. Don't let the bar drag your shoulders open; if you're rotating, lighten the load.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12/side, rotational stability. Noa: Low compressive load on spine. Boris: 3x10/side, pairs well with landmine rotation.

exercises.name: Suitcase Carry
exercises.setupNotes: Pick up a heavy dumbbell or kettlebell in one hand, stand tall, and walk forward at a steady pace. Keep your shoulders level and resist the urge to lean toward the side holding the weight. Your torso should look the same as if you were carrying nothing. Don't let the weight tilt your whole body sideways — if you're leaning noticeably, the weight is too heavy or you're not bracing enough.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 20-40m/side, functional strength. Noa: Safe, excellent for spinal stability asymmetry. Boris: 3 trips/side, heavy and slow.

exercises.name: EZ Bar Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Stand holding an EZ bar at the angled grip (the wider outer grip). Keeping your elbows at your sides, curl the bar up toward your chest, then lower slowly. The angled grip puts your wrists in a more natural position than a straight bar. Don't let your elbows drift forward at the top — keep them pinned to your sides.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, wrist-friendly bicep builder. Noa: Lower wrist stress than straight bar — great for those with wrist issues. Boris: 3x10, heavy and controlled.

exercises.name: EZ Bar Skullcrusher
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on a flat bench holding an EZ bar with an overhand grip, arms extended above your chest. Lower the bar toward your forehead by bending only your elbows, keeping your upper arms vertical, then press back up. The EZ bar is easier on the wrists than a straight bar for this movement. Don't flare your elbows out — keep them pointed at the ceiling.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12 reps, excellent long-head tricep. Noa: Less elbow stress than straight bar. Boris: 3x10, pair with close-grip bench.

exercises.name: Cable Overhead Tricep Extension
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a rope to a high pulley. Turn away from the machine and hold the rope behind your head with both hands. Step forward to create tension, then extend your arms forward and overhead until they're straight, then return slowly. Keep your elbows close to your head. Don't let your elbows drift out wide — they should point forward, not sideways.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, long head tricep emphasis. Noa: Lower wrist stress vs dumbbells. Boris: 3x15, excellent finisher.

exercises.name: Reverse Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a barbell or dumbbells with an overhand (pronated) grip. Keeping your elbows at your sides, curl the weight up toward your shoulders, then lower slowly. The reversed grip shifts work to the brachioradialis and forearms. Don't swing your torso — keep it still and let your forearms do the work.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, thick forearm and brachialis development. Noa: Wrist stability required, use EZ bar if discomfort. Boris: 3x12, pairs with hammer curl.

exercises.name: Rope Tricep Pushdown
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a rope to a high pulley. Grip both ends of the rope with a neutral grip and stand facing the machine. Keeping your elbows pinned to your sides, push the rope down and split the ends apart at the bottom, squeezing your triceps hard. Return slowly. Don't let your elbows flare out — they should stay glued to your sides throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-20 reps, lateral and medial tricep head. Noa: Very safe, no joint stress. Boris: 3x15-20, classic finisher.

exercises.name: Zottman Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing up. Curl them up to shoulder height as you would a normal curl, then at the top rotate your wrists so your palms face down. Lower the dumbbells slowly with the overhand grip, then rotate back to underhand and repeat. Don't rush the wrist rotation — it should be deliberate and controlled.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-12 reps, hits all three elbow flexors. Noa: Moderate forearm stress on eccentric. Boris: 3x10, slow eccentric — 3-4s down.

exercises.name: Tate Press
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on a flat bench holding dumbbells directly above your chest with your palms facing your feet. Bend your elbows outward, lowering the dumbbell ends toward your chest, then press back up by extending your triceps. Unlike skull crushers, your elbows flare out rather than staying in. Don't let the dumbbells touch your chest — stop just short and press back.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-15 reps, unique medial head stimulus. Noa: Low joint stress when elbows tracked correctly. Boris: 3x12, great tricep variety.

exercises.name: Machine Preacher Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Adjust the seat so your upper arms rest flat against the preacher pad. Grip the handles with an underhand grip and curl from the fully extended position up to near the top, squeezing your biceps. Lower slowly to a full stretch. Don't yank the weight up from the bottom — control the full range of motion.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, excellent for peak bicep development. Noa: Safe, fixed path prevents elbow drift. Boris: 3x12, go heavier than free-weight preacher.

exercises.name: Cable Hammer Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a rope to a low pulley. Hold both ends of the rope with a neutral grip (palms facing each other). Keeping your elbows at your sides, curl the rope up toward your shoulders, then lower slowly. The constant cable tension makes this more challenging at the top than dumbbell hammer curls. Don't let your elbows drift forward during the curl.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-15 reps, brachialis thickness builder. Noa: Very safe, wrist-neutral position. Boris: 3x12-15, pairs with supinated curls.

exercises.name: Spider Curl
exercises.setupNotes: Lie face-down on an incline bench set to about 45°. Let your arms hang straight down and hold a barbell or dumbbells. Curl the weight up toward your face, keeping your upper arms perpendicular to the floor, then lower slowly. This position eliminates any chance of using body momentum. Don't let your upper arms swing — they should stay pointing straight down throughout.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-12 reps, high stretch + peak contraction. Noa: Low cheating potential, strict form. Boris: 3x10, excellent bicep peak builder.

exercises.name: JM Press
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on a bench holding a barbell with a medium-close grip, arms extended above your chest. Lower the bar in a path between a skull crusher and a close-grip bench press — the bar comes toward your chin/throat, not just your forehead. Your elbows angle forward slightly. Press back up. Don't let the bar get too close to your throat — this takes practice to find the right groove.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 6-10 reps, mass builder for tricep. Noa: High elbow stress — warm up thoroughly. Boris: 3x8, heavy compound tricep work.

exercises.name: Romanian Deadlift (RDL) - DB
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs, stand tall with a slight knee bend. Push your hips back and lower the dumbbells down your legs, keeping your back straight and chest up. Stop at the point of maximum hamstring stretch, then drive your hips through to return to standing. Don't let the dumbbells drift away from your body — they should stay close to your legs the whole way down.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, greater ROM than BB variant. Noa: Wrist neutral, less lower back demand than BB. Boris: 3x10-12, ideal for unilateral progressions.

exercises.name: Weighted Pull-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Attach a weight plate to a dip belt or hold a dumbbell between your feet, then hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar, lower back down to a full hang, and repeat. The added weight makes this significantly harder — only add weight once you can do 10 clean bodyweight pull-ups. Don't sacrifice form for the sake of more weight — every rep should look as clean as a bodyweight pull-up.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 4-8 reps, strongest lat stimulus. Noa: Ensure shoulder mobility before heavy loading. Boris: 3-5x5 heavy, treat like an upper body deadlift.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Row
exercises.setupNotes: Set an incline bench to about 45 degrees and lie face-down on it with a dumbbell in each hand hanging below you. Row both dumbbells toward your hips simultaneously, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top. Lower back to a full hang. Because your chest is supported, you can't swing — use that to your advantage and really focus on squeezing your back at the top.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15 reps, strict form advantage over dumbbell row. Noa: Very safe, no lower back involvement. Boris: 3x12, great for balanced back development.

exercises.name: Single-Arm Cable Row
exercises.setupNotes: Sit or stand at a low cable and grab the single handle with one hand, arm fully extended. Pull the handle to your side, driving your elbow back and squeezing that side of your back. Let your arm fully extend again with a slight torso rotation for a bigger range of motion. Don't just pull with your arm — think about driving your elbow back and feel the lat doing the work.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 10-15/side, excellent unilateral lat and rhomboid work. Noa: Low spinal load. Boris: 3x12/side, rotate through full range.

exercises.name: Meadows Row
exercises.setupNotes: Set up a landmine or wedge one end of a barbell into a corner, stand perpendicular to it and stagger your feet. Grab the bar at the far end with one hand and row it up toward your hip, letting your torso rotate slightly for range. Lower back down and repeat before switching sides. Don't spin your whole torso to heave the weight — a slight rotation is fine, but it should be controlled and not a full body swing.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-12/side, unique lat + upper back stimulus. Noa: Moderate lower back demand — brace core. Boris: 3x10/side, often forgotten exercise.

exercises.name: Wide-Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.setupNotes: Sit with thighs under the pads and grip the bar with a very wide overhand grip, wider than a standard pulldown. Pull the bar to your upper chest by driving your elbows down and out, squeezing your lats at the bottom. Let your arms extend fully overhead before the next rep. Don't lean excessively backward — a slight lean is part of the movement, but if you're nearly horizontal, go lighter.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, lat width emphasis. Noa: Avoid behind-the-neck variant — cervical spine risk. Boris: 3x10-12, classic pulldown.

exercises.name: Cable Pullover
exercises.setupNotes: Set a high cable with a rope or straight bar and face the machine, stepping back a couple of feet. With arms extended, pull the cable down in an arc from overhead to your hips, squeezing your lats hard at the bottom. Slowly let your arms rise back overhead with control. Don't bend your elbows to help — keep them slightly bent but fixed throughout; this is a lat exercise, not an arm exercise.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 12-15 reps, excellent lat isolation at long length. Noa: Low spinal stress. Boris: 3x15, great lat pump finisher.

exercises.name: Barbell Shrug
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a barbell in front of your thighs with an overhand grip at shoulder width, standing tall. Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible, hold briefly at the top, then lower with control. Keep your arms straight — this is a trap exercise, not a bicep curl. Don't roll your shoulders forward in circles — that adds no benefit and puts your shoulder joints in a bad position.
exercises.committeeNotes: Dr. Brad: 8-15 reps, upper trapezius thickness. Noa: No shoulder rolling — rotator cuff stress. Boris: 3x10 heavy, add straps for grip.

exercises.name: Kneeling Single-Arm Lat Pulldown
exercises.setupNotes: Kneel in front of a cable machine, set the pulley high, and grab the handle with one hand. Pull the handle down toward your hip in an arc, driving your elbow toward the floor and squeezing your lat hard. Let your arm extend fully overhead between reps.
