exercises.setupNotes: Support your straight, horizontal body on locked arms with legs spread wide (straddled) to reduce the lever arm vs. a full planche. Shoulders must stay over or past the hands, hips in line with shoulders, and back flat. The straddle position is the penultimate planche progression — splitting the legs distributes the load and makes it attainable before the full closed-leg version. Target: 5–10 second hold before attempting full planche.

exercises.name: Full Planche Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Hold your entire body horizontal, parallel to the ground, supported only on locked straight arms with legs closed together. The ultimate planche progression — requires years of dedicated straight-arm pushing strength development. Shoulders must be noticeably past the hands, entire body rigid, and there should be zero hip sag or pike. Even a 2-second hold is considered elite.

exercises.name: Planche Push-Up
exercises.setupNotes: From a straddle or full planche hold, lower your body by bending your elbows — maintaining the planche position (body parallel to floor) throughout — then press back up to lockout. One of the most advanced calisthenics pushing exercises. Typically performed for 1–5 reps even at elite level. Begin with straddle planche push-ups before attempting full planche push-ups.

exercises.name: Tuck Front Lever Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a pull-up bar or rings with an overhand grip. Pull your body up until it is horizontal with knees tucked tightly to your chest — your back should be flat and parallel to the ground. Arms are straight or very slightly bent. Hold for time. This is the entry-level front lever progression — the tucked knees shorten the lever significantly. Aim for a solid 10-second hold before progressing to advanced tuck.

exercises.name: Advanced Tuck Front Lever Hold
exercises.setupNotes: From the tuck front lever, extend your hips back so your thighs are parallel to the floor while your knees remain bent at 90 degrees. Back remains flat and horizontal. The additional lever arm demands significantly more lat strength. This is the second major front lever checkpoint — typically takes 3–6 months to achieve from a solid tuck hold.

exercises.name: One-Leg Front Lever Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a front lever position with one leg extended and one knee tucked to your chest. Alternate which leg is extended. The asymmetric position bridges the gap between advanced tuck and straddle front lever. Focus on keeping the body horizontal and preventing rotation. Useful when straddle flexibility is a limiting factor.

exercises.name: Straddle Front Lever Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a front lever with legs spread wide (straddled) to shorten the effective lever arm compared to a full front lever. Body is horizontal, arms straight, hips in line with the torso. The last progression before full front lever. Requires full hip flexor strength to keep legs up and strong lat engagement to keep the torso horizontal. Target: 5-second hold.

exercises.name: Full Front Lever Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a bar or rings and hold your entire body horizontal — body straight, legs closed together, arms locked straight, face pointing down. The full front lever requires exceptional lat and posterior chain strength. Competition minimum is a 2-second hold for recognition. Even seasoned calisthenics athletes take 12–24 months to develop from beginner to full front lever.

exercises.name: Front Lever Row
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a tuck or full front lever hold, then row yourself up toward the bar by bending your elbows — maintaining the horizontal body position throughout — then lower back to the hold position. Often trained in tuck front lever rows to build the pulling strength needed for the full front lever. The dynamic version significantly increases the strength demand vs. the static hold.

exercises.name: Back Lever Hold
exercises.setupNotes: From a dead hang, perform a skin-the-cat to get into a German hang (shoulders in extension), then straighten your body until it is horizontal and parallel to the ground with your back facing up. Hold for time with arms straight. The back lever is easier than the front lever because of the shoulder position — entry progression for rings-based static skills. Always train skin-the-cat first to develop shoulder flexibility.

exercises.name: Skin the Cat
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from rings or a bar with an overhand grip. Keeping your arms straight, lift your feet up and over — rotating your body backward (toes go behind and below you) until you are in a German hang (shoulders fully extended overhead). Hold briefly, then reverse the movement back to the starting position. The skin-the-cat builds the shoulder flexibility and strength needed for the back lever. Essential mobility prerequisite.

exercises.name: Ring L-Sit
exercises.setupNotes: Support yourself on the rings with arms straight down and rings turned out. Lift your legs to horizontal (parallel to the floor) to form an L shape, with legs together and toes pointed. Hold for time. Significantly harder than the parallette or floor version because the rings are unstable — your shoulder stabilizers must work continuously to prevent lateral movement. The ring L-sit is a prerequisite for more advanced ring static skills.

exercises.name: Ring Support Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Mount the rings and hold yourself in a support position — arms fully locked out below you with rings at hip height. Turn the rings out (externally rotate) so your knuckles face forward and your pinkies face out. Hold for time with a tight hollow body position. The ring support hold is the foundational ring skill — it builds the wrist, elbow, and shoulder stability needed for muscle-ups and ring dips. Aim for 30+ seconds before progressing to other ring work.

exercises.name: Iron Cross Hold
exercises.setupNotes: From a ring support hold, lower the rings outward until your arms are extended straight out to the sides — forming a cross with your body. Hold for time at the lowest position where you can maintain control. One of the most demanding ring static skills, requiring exceptional shoulder joint strength. Always use band assistance or a spotter when beginning. Build prerequisite strength with years of ring support work, ring dips, and weighted pull-ups.

exercises.name: One-Arm Pull-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a bar with one hand and pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar, then lower under control. The free hand can rest on your wrist or stay at your side. One of the most demanding single-arm pulling exercises possible — prerequisite is typically 15–20 strict pull-ups plus specific one-arm negative training. Common progression path: assisted one-arm pull-ups, one-arm negatives, then full reps.

exercises.name: One-Arm Negative Pull-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Jump or use a chair to get your chin above the bar, then lower yourself as slowly as possible using only one arm. Aim for 5+ seconds per rep. This eccentric-only approach is the primary training method for developing one-arm pull-up strength before being able to do the concentric phase. Focus on controlling the descent evenly throughout the range.

exercises.name: Human Flag Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Grip a vertical pole with both hands (top hand pushing away, bottom hand pulling in). Hold your body out horizontally to the side — entirely parallel to the ground, with legs together. Hold for time. The human flag is an iconic calisthenics skill requiring enormous lateral core and shoulder strength. Begin with a tuck flag (knees bent) before attempting full straight-body variation.

exercises.name: Tuck Human Flag Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a vertical pole with both hands (top hand pushing, bottom hand pulling) and hold your body out to the side with your knees tucked tightly to your chest. The tuck shortens the lever arm and makes the human flag achievable as a progression. Hold for time. Progress to a straddle flag and then a full flag as strength increases.

exercises.name: V-Sit Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Support yourself on the floor, parallettes, or bars with arms locked out. Lift your legs above the horizontal — past the L-sit — creating a V shape with your body. Legs and torso form an angle greater than 90 degrees. Hold for time. The V-sit bridges the gap between the L-sit and the manna, requiring advanced hip flexor strength and compression ability. Also called a 'V-hold'.

exercises.name: Manna Hold
exercises.setupNotes: Support yourself on straight arms with your legs raised high — ideally past vertical — so your hips are elevated well above shoulder height. The body forms an extreme piked position with legs pointing toward the ceiling. One of the most advanced static positions in gymnastics and calisthenics, requiring extreme hip flexor and compression strength combined with significant posterior shoulder mobility. Progress through L-sit, V-sit, then increasingly vertical leg positions.

exercises.name: Butterfly Pull-Up
exercises.setupNotes: A kipping pull-up variant where the hips drive in a circular arc (forward-up-back) rather than the standard front-back kip. The butterfly technique enables much higher rep rates than standard kipping and is the standard for fast CrossFit metcon sets. Prerequisite: solid kipping pull-up before learning the butterfly cycle. Requires good shoulder mobility and timing.

exercises.name: Burpee Box Jump-Over
exercises.setupNotes: Perform a burpee beside a box, then jump up and over the box landing on the other side. Unlike a standard box jump where you step down, the rep ends on the far side of the box. You can either jump onto the box and off the other side (two jumps) or broad jump directly over it. A CrossFit Open staple — appeared in Open 20.1 and multiple other workouts. The step-over modification is accepted in scaled divisions.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Hang Power Clean
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at hip height (hang position). Hinge slightly, then drive explosively through hips and shrug, pulling the dumbbells to shoulder height with elbows high. Drop under into a partial squat catching the dumbbells in a front-rack position (heads resting on shoulders). Official CrossFit movement — appears in Open programming. Each DB should finish with head resting on shoulder, elbows forward.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand resting on your shoulders in a front-rack position (heads on shoulders, elbows forward). Squat to full depth — hip crease below parallel — keeping your torso as upright as possible and elbows high. Drive through your heels to stand. Official CrossFit movement; the front rack DB position demands significant shoulder mobility and anterior core strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Push Press
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height. Dip slightly at the knees keeping your torso vertical, then drive explosively through your legs and press both dumbbells to full overhead lockout simultaneously. Lower under control. Official CrossFit movement — the dumbbell version is programmed in Open workouts when barbells aren't available and allows independent arm loading.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Push Jerk
exercises.setupNotes: Hold dumbbells at shoulder height. Dip and drive explosively, pressing the dumbbells overhead while simultaneously re-dipping under them to catch at full lockout — then stand to full extension. The re-dip under the bar distinguishes the jerk from the push press. Official CrossFit movement. Requires timing and coordination to catch the dumbbells in a stable overhead position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front-Rack Lunge
exercises.setupNotes: Hold dumbbells on your shoulders in a front-rack position and perform alternating walking lunges. The front-rack position increases the core stability and shoulder endurance demand vs. dumbbells at the side. Official CrossFit movement. Keep elbows high throughout — dropping the elbows collapses the front rack and shifts load to the wrists.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Overhead Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Hold one or two dumbbells locked out overhead with arms straight and actively pressing into the handles. Squat to full depth (hip crease below parallel) while keeping the dumbbells directly over your base of support. The overhead position demands exceptional shoulder stability, thoracic mobility, and ankle flexibility. Official CrossFit movement — commonly used as a warm-up and in skill-based WODs.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Power Clean
exercises.setupNotes: With a dumbbell in each hand, hinge to the floor and perform a full clean pull — driving through your legs and hips, shrugging, and pulling the dumbbells to shoulder height. Unlike the hang power clean, the movement starts from the floor. Each rep catches in a partial squat with DB heads resting on shoulders. Official CrossFit movement.

exercises.name: Snatch Balance
exercises.setupNotes: Start with the bar on your back in snatch-grip width. Push-press the bar overhead and simultaneously drop into a full overhead squat, catching the bar locked out at the bottom. Stand to finish. The snatch balance trains the speed under the bar and receiving position of the snatch. Appears in CF weightlifting programming and skill-focused classes.

exercises.name: Muscle Snatch
exercises.setupNotes: Perform a snatch pull but press the bar overhead without re-bending the knees or dropping under the bar — the bar goes from floor to overhead in a single continuous press with no re-dip. The muscle snatch builds the pulling strength and overhead turnover for the full snatch. Official CrossFit skill-development movement. Typically done at lighter weights than the full snatch.

exercises.name: Hang Snatch
exercises.setupNotes: Starting from a hang position (bar at hip/knee height), perform a full squat snatch — driving through the hips, pulling under, and catching the bar overhead in a full squat. Unlike the hang power snatch (already in DB), the catch is in a full squat below parallel. Official CrossFit movement; excellent for developing snatch timing and hip extension.

exercises.name: Strict Bar Muscle-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Hang from a pull-up bar and perform a muscle-up without any kipping swing — pulling strictly until your hips reach bar height, then pressing to lockout above the bar. Significantly harder than the kipping bar muscle-up. Official CrossFit movement. Requires exceptional pulling strength and the ability to transition smoothly from pull to press at the top.

exercises.name: Chest-to-Wall Handstand Push-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Kick up to a handstand facing the wall so your chest (not back) is toward the wall. Lower your head to the floor and press back up. The chest-to-wall position forces a straighter body line — no hollow back allowed — making this stricter than the standard back-to-wall HSPU. Required for high-level CrossFit competition standards. Prerequisite: solid back-to-wall strict HSPU.

exercises.name: Zercher Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Hold the barbell in the crooks of your elbows, cradled against your forearms and upper arms. Squat to full depth keeping the bar close to your torso and your elbows pointing forward. The anterior loading demands extreme core bracing and upper back strength. Listed on crossfit.com's official movement library. Excellent for developing squat mechanics and core strength.

exercises.name: Slam Ball
exercises.setupNotes: Lift a heavy non-bouncing slam ball (20–100 lb) from the floor to overhead, then explosively throw it straight down into the floor. Pick it up and repeat. Unlike the medicine ball slam (which uses a lighter bouncing ball), a slam ball is dense and designed to absorb impact — it will not bounce back. Common in CF metcons as a full-body power endurance exercise.

exercises.name: Tire Flip
exercises.setupNotes: Stand facing a large tractor tire. Squat low and grip the underside of the tire with both hands, driving it up and forward by extending your hips and legs explosively (like a deadlift-to-push). As the tire rises, transition to a pushing position and drive it up and over onto its other face. A CrossFit garage gym and functional fitness staple. Requires a large tire — typically 200–600+ lb. Drive through your hips first, then push — do not pull with your lower back.

exercises.name: Man Maker
exercises.setupNotes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand in push-up position. Perform a push-up, then row each dumbbell to your hip one at a time, then jump your feet in and stand (or clean the DBs) to perform a thruster. One rep = push-up + row R + row L + thruster. A notorious CrossFit metcon movement that combines every major movement pattern into one rep. Typically done at lighter weights for conditioning.

exercises.name: Yoke Walk
exercises.setupNotes: Carry a yoke (a weighted frame resting across the upper back and shoulders) for a prescribed distance or time. The yoke typically loads 1.5–3× bodyweight and is carried in a similar position to a high-bar squat but while walking. A strongman movement that appears in CF-influenced fitness competitions and garage gym programming. Requires keeping the core braced and taking controlled steps — wobbling accelerates fatigue.

exercises.name: Treadmill Walk
exercises.setupNotes: Set the treadmill to a comfortable walking speed (2.5–4 mph) and step on once the belt is moving. Walk with an upright posture, arms swinging naturally, and avoid holding the handrails — gripping the rails reduces calorie burn and promotes poor posture. Keep your gaze forward rather than looking down at your feet.
exercises.committeeNotes: LISS baseline cardio. Good for active recovery and general aerobic conditioning.

exercises.name: Treadmill Jog
exercises.setupNotes: Set the treadmill to a jogging pace (4–6 mph). Land with a midfoot strike and avoid over-striding — your foot should land roughly under your centre of mass. Keep your shoulders relaxed, arms bent at ~90°, and maintain a slight forward lean from your ankles rather than your waist.
exercises.committeeNotes: Aerobic base building. Sustainable conversational pace.

exercises.name: Treadmill Sprint
exercises.setupNotes: Set the treadmill to a high speed (8–12 mph or beyond). Straddle the belt with feet on the side rails, let the belt reach full speed, then step on and drive hard with both legs. Sprint for the prescribed duration, then step off to the side rails to rest — do not slow the machine down between intervals. Stay light on your feet and drive your knees forward aggressively.
exercises.committeeNotes: HIIT sprint intervals. Typically 10–30 seconds on, 30–90 seconds off.

exercises.name: Treadmill Incline Walk
exercises.setupNotes: Set the treadmill incline to 10–15% and walk at 3–4 mph. Do not hold the handrails — letting go forces your posterior chain and core to stabilise on the slope, which is where most of the training benefit comes from. Lean very slightly into the incline and drive your heel into the belt on each step.
exercises.committeeNotes: Low-impact glute and hamstring emphasis. Popular as the '12-3-30' protocol (12% incline, 3.0 mph, 30 min).

exercises.name: Treadmill Incline Run
exercises.setupNotes: Set the treadmill to a moderate incline (4–8%) and run at a pace that is 1–2 mph slower than your flat-ground pace. Drive your knees up more than on flat terrain and push through your glutes on each step. Don't lean forward excessively — a slight forward lean is fine but avoid bending at the waist.
exercises.committeeNotes: Increases calorie burn and posterior chain engagement vs flat running. Good for hill training without outdoor terrain.

exercises.name: Stationary Bike (calories)
exercises.setupNotes: Adjust the seat so your knee has a slight bend at the bottom of the pedal stroke — a fully extended leg means the seat is too high. Set the resistance so you can maintain a cadence of 70–100 RPM without bouncing in the seat. Sit upright with a light grip on the handlebars and drive the pedal stroke through the entire circle rather than just pushing down.
exercises.committeeNotes: Low-impact aerobic conditioning. Calorie target tracked on the console.

exercises.name: Stationary Bike (minutes)
exercises.setupNotes: Same setup as calorie-based stationary bike — seat height adjusted for a slight knee bend at the bottom of the stroke. For time-based sessions, hold a consistent effort level. Zone 2 cardio is typically 60–70% of max heart rate and should feel like you could hold a conversation. Vary your resistance if you want to simulate interval efforts.
exercises.committeeNotes: Duration-based aerobic conditioning. Zone 2 target: 60–70% max HR.

exercises.name: Spin Bike
exercises.setupNotes: Set both seat height (slight knee bend at bottom) and handlebar height (roughly level with the seat for performance, or higher for comfort). The weighted flywheel on a spin bike gives a smoother, more road-like feel than a regular stationary bike. Resistance is controlled by a dial or knob — crank it up for climbs and back off for sprints. Seated and standing climbing positions both have their place.
exercises.committeeNotes: Indoor cycling cardio, typically used in group fitness classes or interval sessions.

exercises.name: Outdoor Cycling
exercises.setupNotes: Ride outdoors on a road or trail bike. Keep a cadence of 80–100 RPM for road cycling and shift gears to maintain this cadence across different gradients. Stay aerodynamic on flat roads — lower your torso and keep your elbows bent. Climb seated with a slightly higher gear or stand for shorter, steep climbs. Log by duration or distance.
exercises.committeeNotes: Outdoor aerobic conditioning. Session effort depends heavily on terrain and weather.

exercises.name: Rowing (watts)
exercises.setupNotes: Same rowing technique as distance or calorie variants. Switch the monitor display to watts mode. Watts are a real-time power output measure — they respond instantly to your effort level, making them useful for interval training where you want to hit a specific power target each rep. Hold your target watt number for the prescribed duration.
exercises.committeeNotes: Power-based rowing. 1.5W per kg body weight is a strong recreational benchmark.

exercises.name: Ski Erg (watts)
exercises.setupNotes: Same technique as other Ski Erg variants. Set the Concept2 monitor to watts/power mode. Use watt targets to programme interval sessions — hold a target wattage for a work period then recover. Watts respond instantly to your effort so you can quickly see if you're above or below your target.
exercises.committeeNotes: Power-based Ski Erg intervals. Useful for structured interval programming.

exercises.name: Assault Bike (watts)
exercises.setupNotes: Same full-body assault bike technique. Switch the monitor to watts mode and target a specific power output for each work interval. The Assault Bike fan creates resistance proportional to your speed, so the harder you push, the more resistance you face — this makes holding a watt target very demanding at high outputs.
exercises.committeeNotes: Power-based Assault Bike intervals. Elite CrossFitters target 400–600W+ on 10-second sprints.

exercises.name: Echo Bike (calories)
exercises.setupNotes: The Echo Bike (Rogue) is functionally identical to the Assault Bike — mount, grab the moving handles, and drive with both arms and legs simultaneously. Lean slightly forward and keep your core braced. The fan creates air resistance that scales with effort. Pace calorie efforts by targeting a consistent stroke rate rather than going all-out and fading.
exercises.committeeNotes: Air bike variant from Rogue. Used in CrossFit gyms and home setups. Calories are the standard metric.

exercises.name: Echo Bike (watts)
exercises.setupNotes: Same technique as Echo Bike calories. Switch the monitor to watts mode for power-based interval training. The Echo Bike console tracks instantaneous and average power output. Use this for structured intervals where you want to hit specific watt targets each rep.
exercises.committeeNotes: Power-based Echo Bike intervals. Same effort profile as Assault Bike watts.

exercises.name: Triple Under
exercises.setupNotes: Hold the rope handles at hip height with elbows close to your body. Jump higher than a double under and flick the wrists even faster so the rope passes under your feet three times before you land. This requires maximum wrist speed and significant jump height. Start with consistent double unders before attempting triples — the timing window is very narrow and the learning curve is steep.
exercises.committeeNotes: Advanced jump rope skill. Rarely programmed but a benchmark of rope mastery. 1 triple under = significant conditioning stimulus.

exercises.name: Speed Rope (calories)
exercises.setupNotes: Use a lightweight speed rope and jump at a rapid, sustainable cadence. Single unders at high speed (160–180 RPM) are surprisingly demanding. Keep elbows at your sides, rotate only at the wrists, and land softly on the balls of your feet. If you're tracking calories, use a calorie counter app or estimate based on duration and bodyweight.
exercises.committeeNotes: High-cadence single under cardio. Calorie-based tracking. Good warm-up or conditioning finisher.

exercises.name: Freestyle Swim
exercises.setupNotes: Enter the pool and settle into your lane. For freestyle (front crawl): rotate your body 45° with each stroke, reach your lead arm forward before pulling, turn your head to breathe every 2 or 3 strokes, and maintain a steady kick from the hips rather than the knees. Efficient freestyle is about minimising drag — keep your hips high and your body line flat.
exercises.committeeNotes: The fastest and most common competitive swim stroke. Log by distance (laps/metres) or duration.

exercises.name: Backstroke Swim
exercises.setupNotes: Float on your back and alternate arm pulls in a windmill motion — one arm exits and sweeps back while the other pulls through the water. Rotate your body slightly with each stroke, keep your hips near the surface, and flutter kick continuously from the hips. Breathing is unrestricted since your face is out of the water. Navigate by counting strokes or checking flags above the lane lines.
exercises.committeeNotes: Only competitive stroke swum on the back. Good shoulder mobility and lat activation. Log by laps or duration.

exercises.name: Breaststroke Swim
exercises.setupNotes: Pull both arms forward together and sweep them out and back in a circular motion while your legs perform a frog kick. The timing is: pull → breathe (head rises) → kick → glide. The glide phase is critical for efficiency — don't rush it. Breaststroke is the slowest competitive stroke but is very accessible and easy on the joints.
exercises.committeeNotes: The slowest competitive stroke. Low impact on the knees if frog kick is performed correctly. Log by laps or duration.

exercises.name: Butterfly Swim
exercises.setupNotes: Both arms recover over the water simultaneously and pull through together in an hourglass pattern. Hips undulate in a dolphin kick — two kicks per arm cycle. Breathe every other stroke to avoid raising your head too high. Butterfly is the most technically demanding and physically taxing stroke, requiring excellent hip flexibility and shoulder strength.
exercises.committeeNotes: The most demanding competitive stroke. Very high energy cost. Log by laps — typically done in short sets.

exercises.name: Pool Laps
exercises.setupNotes: Swim continuous laps in a pool using any stroke. A standard Olympic pool lap is 50m; a standard training pool is 25m (1 length) or 50m (1 lap). Alternate strokes to distribute fatigue, or stick to one stroke for a focused session. Log by total laps, total metres, or duration.
exercises.committeeNotes: General swimming cardio. Use this when stroke type is mixed or unspecified.

exercises.name: Elliptical (minutes)
exercises.setupNotes: Step onto the elliptical and grip the moving handles. Drive the pedals in a smooth oval motion, pushing and pulling the handles alternately with your arms. Keep your heels in contact with the footpads throughout the stride — don't rise up on your toes. Set a resistance and ramp level that lets you maintain 55–65 RPM with moderate effort. Don't lean heavily on the handles; let your legs carry most of the work.
exercises.committeeNotes: Low-impact full body cardio. Duration-based. Excellent for joint-friendly aerobic conditioning.

exercises.name: Elliptical (calories)
exercises.setupNotes: Same technique as the duration-based elliptical. Chase a calorie target on the console rather than a time. Calorie estimates on ellipticals are typically inflated vs true expenditure, so treat the number as a relative effort target rather than an absolute calorie count.
exercises.committeeNotes: Calorie-based elliptical cardio. Console estimates are approximate — use as a relative effort metric.

exercises.name: Stair Climber (minutes)
exercises.setupNotes: Step onto the stair climber and begin at a slow speed to find your footing. Place your full foot on each step — heel included — and drive up through your glutes rather than your calves. Do not lean heavily on the handrails; you should be able to rest your fingertips lightly for balance. Set a pace where you're taking deliberate, controlled steps rather than tiny shuffle steps.
exercises.committeeNotes: High glute and quad activation. Low impact vs running. Duration-based session.

exercises.name: Stair Climber (floors)
exercises.setupNotes: Same technique as time-based stair climbing. Track floors climbed on the console — most machines define 1 floor as approximately 10 steps. Drive through your whole foot on each step and squeeze your glutes at the top. Aim for a consistent floor-per-minute rate throughout your session.
exercises.committeeNotes: Floor-based stair climber metric. A standard benchmark is 50+ floors in 30 minutes.

exercises.name: Hiking
exercises.setupNotes: Walk on trails or uneven outdoor terrain at a moderate pace. Wear appropriate footwear with ankle support. Use trekking poles on steep descents to reduce knee load. Keep your weight slightly forward when ascending and plant your heel first when descending. Log by duration or total distance.
exercises.committeeNotes: Outdoor low-intensity steady-state cardio. Elevation gain increases intensity significantly.

exercises.name: Rucking
exercises.setupNotes: Load a backpack with weight plates or sandbags (10–50 lbs depending on your level) and walk at a brisk pace. Keep your shoulders pulled back and your core tight — the rucksack will try to pull you into a forward lean. Walk on flat terrain to start, then progress to hills. GORUCK recommends 1/3 of bodyweight as a challenging load.
exercises.committeeNotes: Military-origin loaded hiking. Outstanding for posterior chain, grip, and aerobic base. Log by distance or duration.

exercises.name: LISS Cardio
exercises.setupNotes: Perform any steady-state cardio modality (walking, cycling, swimming, elliptical) at a low-to-moderate intensity for 30–60+ minutes. Target 60–70% of your maximum heart rate — you should be able to hold a full conversation throughout. LISS stands for Low-Intensity Steady State. Use this as an active recovery session or for fat-loss programming on non-lifting days.
exercises.committeeNotes: Protocol entry — log any LISS session here when modality is mixed or unspecified. Duration is the primary metric.

exercises.name: HIIT Cardio
exercises.setupNotes: Alternate between short high-intensity work intervals (20–40 seconds) and recovery periods (10–60 seconds). A classic HIIT protocol is 20s on / 10s off × 8 rounds (Tabata). During work intervals, push to 85–95% of max heart rate. During rest, actively recover — don't stop moving entirely. Log the number of rounds and the work:rest ratio used.
exercises.committeeNotes: Protocol entry — log any HIIT session here when modality is mixed or unspecified. Note work:rest ratio in the set notes.

exercises.name: Depth Jump
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on a box (12–24 inches). Step off (don't jump off) and land softly on both feet simultaneously. The moment you touch the ground, immediately explode into a maximum-height vertical jump. The key is minimising ground contact time — think of the ground as a hot surface. Use a lower box to start; ground contact should be under 0.25 seconds once you're proficient.
exercises.committeeNotes: Reactive strength / SSC training.

exercises.name: Jump Squat
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Lower into a squat until your thighs are parallel to the floor, then explode upward as high as possible. Land softly with bent knees and immediately flow into the next rep. Keep your chest up throughout and avoid letting your knees cave inward on the landing.
exercises.committeeNotes: Lower-body explosive power.

exercises.name: Plyo Push-Up
exercises.setupNotes: Start in a push-up position. Lower your chest to the floor under control, then press explosively so your hands leave the ground. Land with soft elbows and immediately lower into the next rep. Keep your core tight throughout so your hips don't sag. Progress from knee plyo push-ups if you can't yet achieve hand lift-off from a full position.
exercises.committeeNotes: Upper-body plyometric power.

exercises.name: Lateral Bound
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on one foot. Push laterally off that foot, driving your knee up, and bound as far sideways as possible, landing on the opposite foot. Stick the landing for a beat, then immediately bound back. Focus on a powerful single-leg push and a controlled single-leg landing — don't let the landing knee collapse inward.
exercises.committeeNotes: Single-leg lateral power and deceleration.

exercises.name: Hurdle Jump
exercises.setupNotes: Stand facing a hurdle (or cone). Jump with both feet simultaneously, driving your knees up to clear the obstacle, and land softly on the other side. Immediately rebound into the next hurdle. Keep ground contact time short and stay on the balls of your feet. Set hurdle height appropriately — start low and progress as mechanics improve.
exercises.committeeNotes: Multi-directional plyo power.

exercises.name: Hip Flexor Stretch
exercises.setupNotes: Kneel on one knee with the other foot forward in a lunge position. Drive your hips forward and slightly tuck your pelvis until you feel a stretch in the front of the kneeling-leg hip. Raise the same-side arm overhead to deepen. Hold 30–60 seconds per side. Don't arch your lower back — the stretch should be felt in the hip, not the spine.
exercises.committeeNotes: Targets iliopsoas and rectus femoris.

exercises.name: Hamstring Stretch
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the floor with one leg extended and the other foot tucked against your inner thigh. Hinge forward from your hips (not your waist), reaching your hands toward your foot until you feel a stretch down the back of the extended leg. Hold 30–60 seconds per side. Keep your back flat — rounding through the spine turns this into a back stretch, not a hamstring stretch.
exercises.committeeNotes: Seated single-leg hamstring stretch.

exercises.name: Quad Stretch
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on one foot (use a wall for balance if needed). Bend the other knee and grab the ankle behind you, pulling your heel toward your glute. Keep your knees together and stand tall — don't let the stretching-side knee drift outward or tilt your pelvis forward. Hold 30–60 seconds per side.
exercises.committeeNotes: Standing quadriceps / rectus femoris stretch.

exercises.name: Child's Pose
exercises.setupNotes: Kneel on the floor, sit your hips back toward your heels, and extend both arms forward along the floor. Let your forehead rest on the mat. Breathe deeply and let your lower back, lats, and hips sink toward the floor. Hold 30–90 seconds. Walk your hands to one side to increase the lateral trunk stretch.
exercises.committeeNotes: General spinal decompression and lat/hip stretch.

exercises.name: Pigeon Pose
exercises.setupNotes: From a plank, bring one knee forward and place the shin diagonally across the mat (knee near the same-side hand, foot near the opposite hand). Lower your hips toward the floor. If your hips don't reach, place a folded blanket under the hip of the front leg. Walk your hands forward and rest your forehead on the floor. Hold 1–2 minutes per side. Keep the front foot flexed to protect the knee.
exercises.committeeNotes: Deep external hip rotator and glute stretch.

exercises.name: Downward Dog
exercises.setupNotes: Start on all fours. Tuck your toes under and push your hips up and back until your body forms an inverted V-shape. Press through the palms to push the floor away, lengthen your spine, and work your heels toward the floor (they don't need to touch). Hold 30–60 seconds, pedalling one heel at a time if your calves are tight. Keep your neck relaxed.
exercises.committeeNotes: Full-chain posterior stretch — hamstrings, calves, spine, lats.

exercises.name: Cat-Cow
exercises.setupNotes: Kneel on all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips. Inhale: drop your belly toward the floor, lift your chest and tailbone (Cow). Exhale: round your spine toward the ceiling, tuck your chin and pelvis (Cat). Move slowly and breathe into each position. 8–12 slow reps. This is a mobility drill, not a static hold — pace it with your breath.
exercises.committeeNotes: Spinal flexion-extension mobility warm-up or cooldown.

exercises.name: Shoulder Cross-Body Stretch
exercises.setupNotes: Bring one arm straight across your chest. Use your opposite hand or forearm to press it gently into your body until you feel a stretch in the rear of the shoulder. Hold 30–60 seconds per side. Keep the stretching shoulder down — don't let it shrug up toward your ear.
exercises.committeeNotes: Posterior deltoid and rotator cuff stretch.

exercises.name: Tricep Stretch
exercises.setupNotes: Raise one arm overhead and bend the elbow so your hand drops behind your head. Use the other hand to gently push the elbow further back and down. Hold 30–60 seconds per side. Keep your ribcage neutral — don't flare it out as you lift the arm.
exercises.committeeNotes: Overhead tricep / lat stretch.

exercises.name: Seated Spinal Twist
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the floor with both legs extended. Cross one foot over the opposite knee and plant it flat on the floor. Hug the raised knee with the opposite arm and rotate toward it, placing your other hand on the floor behind you for support. On each inhale, sit taller; on each exhale, rotate a little further. Hold 30–60 seconds per side.
exercises.committeeNotes: Thoracic and lumbar rotational stretch.

exercises.name: Standing Calf Stretch
exercises.setupNotes: Stand facing a wall. Place the ball of one foot against the wall with the heel on the floor. Lean forward gently until you feel a deep stretch in the calf. Hold 30–60 seconds per side. Straightening the knee targets the gastrocnemius; slightly bending it shifts the stretch to the soleus. Do both variations.
exercises.committeeNotes: Gastrocnemius and soleus stretch.

exercises.name: Chest Opener Stretch
exercises.setupNotes: Stand in a doorway (or clasp your hands behind your back). Open both arms out to the sides at shoulder height, press gently into the door frame, and step one foot forward until you feel a stretch across your chest and the fronts of your shoulders. Hold 30–60 seconds. Vary the height of your arms (low / mid / high) to target different fibres of the pectoral muscle.
exercises.committeeNotes: Pectoralis major / anterior deltoid stretch.

exercises.name: Warrior I
exercises.setupNotes: Step one foot forward into a lunge, back foot angled 45 degrees and pressing flat to the floor. Bend the front knee to 90 degrees, square your hips forward, and raise both arms overhead with palms facing each other. Lift your chest and gaze slightly upward. Hold 30–60 seconds per side. Don't let the front knee drift past the toes or collapse inward.
exercises.committeeNotes: Hip flexor, quad, and thoracic extension opener.

exercises.name: Warrior II
exercises.setupNotes: Step your feet wide apart (about 4 feet). Turn your front foot 90 degrees and back foot 15 degrees. Bend the front knee to 90 degrees, keeping it stacked over the ankle. Extend both arms out to the sides at shoulder height and gaze over your front fingers. Sink your hips down and keep your torso upright. Hold 30–60 seconds per side. Don't let the front knee collapse inward.
exercises.committeeNotes: Hip abductor, groin, and lateral hip stretch.

exercises.name: Warrior III
exercises.setupNotes: Stand on one leg. Hinge forward from the hip, extending the other leg straight behind you until your body forms a T-shape parallel to the floor. Arms can reach forward, out to the sides, or back along the body. Flex the back foot and keep the hips level. Hold 20–40 seconds per side. Keep a slight bend in the standing knee — locking it out stresses the joint.
exercises.committeeNotes: Single-leg balance, hip extension, posterior chain lengthening.

exercises.name: Triangle Pose
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet wide apart, front foot turned 90 degrees, back foot 15 degrees. Extend arms out to the sides, then hinge sideways over the front leg, lowering your front hand to your shin or the floor. Extend the top arm straight up, stacking it over the bottom. Open your chest and look up at your top hand. Hold 30–60 seconds per side. Don't bend the front knee — keep both legs straight.
exercises.committeeNotes: Lateral trunk, hip adductor, and hamstring stretch.

exercises.name: Bridge Pose
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back with knees bent, feet flat on the floor hip-width apart. Press your feet and arms into the floor, and lift your hips toward the ceiling. Interlace your fingers underneath you and roll your shoulders under, pressing your arms into the mat to open the chest. Hold 30–60 seconds. Keep your thighs parallel — don't let the knees splay outward.
exercises.committeeNotes: Spine extension, hip flexor, and chest opener.

exercises.name: Cobra Pose
exercises.setupNotes: Lie face down with hands under your shoulders, elbows tucked close to the body. Press through your palms and lift your chest off the floor, straightening your arms only as far as your back can comfortably extend. Keep your hips, thighs, and tops of feet pressed into the mat. Hold 20–40 seconds. Don't shrug your shoulders up to your ears — keep them down and back.
exercises.committeeNotes: Anterior trunk and lumbar extension stretch.

exercises.name: Supine Spinal Twist
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back. Draw one knee into your chest, then guide it across your body with the opposite hand until it rests toward the floor on the other side of you. Extend the same-side arm out to the side and look in the opposite direction to your knee. Keep both shoulders on the floor as much as possible. Hold 60–90 seconds per side.
exercises.committeeNotes: Thoracic and lumbar rotation, glute, and IT band release.

exercises.name: Lizard Pose
exercises.setupNotes: From a low lunge, place the front foot to the outside of the same-side hand. Lower the back knee to the floor. Walk both forearms to the floor inside the front foot for a deeper stretch. Hold 60–90 seconds per side. Keep your back leg long and active — don't let the hips sag. This is a deep hip flexor and groin opener; approach gradually.
exercises.committeeNotes: Deep hip flexor, groin, and hip rotator stretch.

exercises.name: Reclined Butterfly
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back. Bring the soles of your feet together and let your knees fall out to the sides. Rest your arms alongside your body or on your inner thighs with light downward pressure. Allow gravity to open your hips — don't force the knees toward the floor. Hold 60–120 seconds. This is a restorative pose; focus on breathing and letting the inner thighs passively release.
exercises.committeeNotes: Passive inner thigh, groin, and hip external rotator stretch.

exercises.name: Standing Forward Fold
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Hinge forward from the hips, letting your torso hang over your legs. Grab opposite elbows, let the head hang heavy, and bend your knees slightly if your hamstrings are tight. Hold 30–60 seconds. To deepen, straighten the legs gradually — but keep the emphasis on a flat spine hinging at the hip, not rounding through the lower back.
exercises.committeeNotes: Hamstring, calf, and spinal decompression stretch.

exercises.name: Low Lunge
exercises.setupNotes: Step one foot forward into a deep lunge, lower the back knee to the floor. Sink your hips toward the mat and hold upright with hands on the front thigh or raise both arms overhead. Keep the front knee directly over the ankle. Hold 45–60 seconds per side. Tuck the pelvis slightly to protect the lower back and maximise the hip flexor stretch.
exercises.committeeNotes: Hip flexor and psoas stretch; also opens front ankle.

exercises.name: Happy Baby
exercises.setupNotes: Lie on your back. Draw both knees toward your chest, then open them out toward your armpits. Grab the outer edges of your feet (or your ankles or shins) and gently pull your feet downward, stacking your ankles over your knees. Rock gently side to side if that feels good. Hold 60–90 seconds. Keep your lower back pressed gently to the floor — don't let your tailbone lift.
exercises.committeeNotes: Hip external rotation, inner thigh, and lower back release.

exercises.name: Legs Up the Wall
exercises.setupNotes: Sit with one hip close to a wall, then swing your legs up the wall as you lie back. Scoot your hips as close to the wall as is comfortable. Arms rest at your sides, palms up. Hold 2–5 minutes. This is a restorative inversion — it drains fluid from the legs, passively lengthens the hamstrings, and calms the nervous system. If your hamstrings are very tight, move a few inches away from the wall.
exercises.committeeNotes: Passive hamstring stretch; restorative inversion for leg recovery.

exercises.name: Seated Forward Fold
exercises.setupNotes: Sit on the floor with both legs extended straight in front of you. Inhale to lengthen your spine, then hinge forward from the hips reaching your hands toward your feet. Hold your shins, ankles, or feet — wherever you reach without rounding excessively through the mid-back. Hold 45–90 seconds. Use a folded blanket under your hips if your pelvis tips backward when sitting upright.
exercises.committeeNotes: Bilateral hamstring and calf stretch with spinal lengthening.

exercises.name: Wide-Legged Forward Fold
exercises.setupNotes: Stand with feet spread wide (3–5 feet). Hinge forward from the hips with a flat back, placing your hands on the floor or a block between your feet. Walk your hands back between your legs to deepen. Hold 45–60 seconds. Keep a micro-bend in the knees to protect the joint. If you feel pressure in the lower back, widen your stance or place your hands on blocks.
exercises.committeeNotes: Adductor, hamstring, and groin stretch with spinal decompression.
